The question describes a monopolistically competitive industry where existing firms are earning above-normal profits. This implies that the industry is currently operating in a state where firms have a significant market power or are operating in a way that allows them to earn excess profits.

Step 2: Analyze the effect of new entrants.
When new firms enter an industry that is currently operating above its normal profit level, the existing firms face increased competition. This increased competition typically leads to a decrease in the market share of the existing firms and a corresponding decrease in their ability to earn excess profits.

Step 3: Analyze the effect of increased competition on costs.
In a monopolistically competitive industry, firms often have some degree of market power that allows them to earn above-normal profits. This market power often translates into the ability to charge higher prices or maintain high margins. When new firms enter, they typically challenge this market power. To maintain their profits, existing firms must either lower their prices (and thus lower their margins) or face a loss of market share. If they lower their prices, their costs of production (or the cost of their product) will tend to decrease to remain competitive in the face of new entrants.

Step 4: Evaluate the options based on the analysis.
When new firms enter a monopolistically competitive industry where existing firms are earning above-normal profits, the increased competition forces existing firms to lower their prices. To lower their prices, their costs will tend to decrease.
